results.title: A Survey on LLM-as-a-Judge
results.snippet:
\mathcal{R}_{\text{}}: The evaluation explicitly designed to ensure consistency, robustness, and alignment with human judgment.
This reliability is verified through additional validation, calibration, and standardization steps beyond the basic pipeline.
...
f_{\text{R}}: A series of constraints and validation methods applied systematically to the basic LLM-as-a-Judge framework to enhance evaluation reliability.
These include methods to mitigate biases, control variability, and confirm robustness against adversarial inputs.

results.title: LLM as a Judge: The Complete Guide | Galtea Blog
results.snippet:
The article explains the three core judging modes, where LLM judges work well, where they fail, how to write reliable rubrics, and why calibration against a labelled gold set is mandatory before production use.
...
The MT-Bench paper from Zheng et al.
(2023) showed that GPT-4 agrees with human experts at roughly 80%, on par with how often two humans agree with each other on the same task.
...
Use single-answer scoring with an explicit rubric for production evaluation, pairwise comparison for ranking model versions, and never trust a judge you have not measured against a labelled gold set.
...
- ‍**Pairwise comparison** asks the judge to pick which of two responses is better.
This is what Chatbot Arena is built on, and it is the most reliable mode in the MT-Bench results, because relative judgments are easier than absolute ones.
Use pairwise when you are A/B testing two model versions or two prompts.
Do not use it when you need an absolute quality threshold, because pairwise scores have no ground floor: response B can win every comparison and still be unusable.**‍**
-
**Single-answer grading with a rubric** asks the judge to score one response against an explicit standard.
...
The way to find out before deployment is calibration: run the judge against a labelled gold set, measure the gap between its verdicts and the human labels, and iterate the prompt until the gap closes.
The minimum viable calibration loop has three inputs.
A gold set of 30 to 200 examples drawn from the actual evaluation distribution, labelled by domain experts (not generalist annotators; domain knowledge matters here, and the difference is large).
A baseline run that records the full ensemble of accuracy, precision, recall, F1, Pearson correlation, Spearman correlation, and Cohen's Kappa, not just one of them.
And an optimization step that rewrites the prompt against the worst-performing examples, using a meta-LLM as the optimizer (the OPRO pattern from Yang et al. 2023).
The loop iterates until alignment plateaus, which in practice is between five and ten iterations.
...
A gold set is a collection of input-output pairs labelled by domain experts with the verdict you want the judge to produce.
Three properties make a gold set load-bearing for evaluation.
It has to be drawn from the actual evaluation distribution: real queries, real retrieved contexts, real agent responses, not synthetic examples that look plausible to whoever wrote the dataset.
...
Batching requests so judge inference does not dominate end-to-end latency.
Retry handling for the cases where the judge returns malformed JSON, exceeds rate limits, or times out.
Structured logging of verdicts and reasoning traces so you can diagnose later why a specific case was flagged.
Regression detection that fires when alignment on the canary gold set drops between deploys.
Versioning of judge prompts the same way you version the application code, so you can attribute alignment changes to specific prompt edits.
...
When the task has a deterministic correctness check, do not use a judge.
SQL query equivalence, JSON schema validation, regex matching, exact-string comparison, and tool-call argument matching are all solved problems.
An LLM judge wrapping any of these adds latency, cost, and a non-zero chance of being wrong about something that has a definitive answer.
Run the deterministic check, fail fast on mismatch, and only call the judge for the parts that genuinely require natural-language reasoning.
When you have not built a labelled gold set, do not use a judge in production.
The judge gives you a number.
...
The gold set does not need to be enormous (30 well-chosen examples beats 300 sloppy ones), but it has to exist.
When the cost of a missed failure exceeds the cost of human review, do not use a judge as the final gate.
Use it as a triage filter that cuts the volume of cases humans review.

results.title: LLM-as-Judge 2026: When Automated Agent Scoring Reliably ...
results.snippet:
By using a powerful frontier model as an automated scorer, teams can evaluate thousands of agent outputs per hour at a fraction of the cost of human annotation.
But the approach carries real risks: grade inflation, self-preference bias, verbosity gaming, and position artifacts that can make a mediocre agent look production-ready.
...
The setup is conceptually simple: instead of asking a human "is this agent output good?", you ask another LLM.
The judge receives the original input, the agent's output, optional context (retrieved documents, tool call history), and a scoring rubric.
It returns a score, usually 1-5, plus a chain-of-thought rationale explaining the verdict.
This basic pattern has several variants:
- **Pointwise scoring**: Judge evaluates one output against a rubric.
Best for production monitoring where you need absolute quality signals.
- **Reference-based scoring**: Judge compares the output to a gold-standard answer.
Reduces variability for tasks with knowable correct outputs (factual QA, code correctness).
- **Pairwise comparison**: Judge sees two outputs for the same input and picks the better one.
Higher reliability than pointwise for subtle quality differences, but requires 2x inference.
...
The central empirical claim for LLM-as-Judge comes from Zheng et al.'s widely cited NeurIPS 2023 study: GPT-4 achieves **over 80% agreement with human evaluators** — comparable to inter-annotator agreement between two humans evaluating the same output.
In 2025-2026, that baseline has held up for strong judge models (GPT-5 class, Claude Opus 4.x) on well-defined evaluation dimensions.
Pearson correlation coefficients reach 0.85 versus human ground truth in extractive QA and summarization tasks.
But the aggregate number obscures significant variation by task type.
Here is what the research shows:
|Output Type|LLM Judge Reliability|Notes|
|--|--|--|
|Factual accuracy (verifiable)|High (0.82-0.90 correlation)|Reference-based setup required|
|Code correctness (with tests)|High (0.80-0.88)|Reasoning models (o3-class) outperform standard instruction-tuned judges|
|Task completion (binary)|High (0.78-0.85)|Binary framing reduces scale-related noise|
|Summarization quality|Moderate (0.65-0.78)|Verbosity bias inflates scores for longer summaries|
|Instruction following|Moderate (0.70-0.80)|Depends heavily on rubric precision|
|Creative quality|Low-moderate (0.50-0.65)|High variance; humans disagree with each other too|
|Subjective helpfulness|Low (0.45-0.60)|Most susceptible to model-specific style preferences|
The pattern is clear: objective outputs with deterministic ground truth judge reliably.
Subjective outputs — creative writing quality, helpfulness, tone — produce noisy signals that should not gate production deployment decisions without human calibration.
...
### 1.
Verbosity Bias (~15% score inflation)
LLM judges consistently rate longer responses higher, independent of quality.
A 400-word response receives approximately 15% higher scores than a 150-word response containing identical information.
The fix: use explicit 1-4 scales (not 1-10) and add rubric language that rewards conciseness.
...
### 2.
Position Bias (10%+ accuracy shift in pairwise)
In pairwise comparisons, the response presented first receives preferential treatment.
Studies on code evaluation found that simply swapping the order of two responses changes the judge's verdict in more than 10% of cases.
Mitigation: always run pairwise comparisons in both orders and only count verdicts that are consistent across both orderings.
This doubles inference cost but is essential for high-stakes comparisons.
...
Self-Preference Bias (5-7% score boost)
When the same model family is used as both generator and judge, outputs from that model receive a 5-7% scoring premium over objectively equivalent outputs from other models.
...
### 4.
Agreeableness Bias (high false positive rate)
Some judge models exhibit over-acceptance — rating most outputs as satisfactory regardless of actual quality.
Research found true positive rates exceeding 96% with true negative rates below 25% in class-imbalanced settings.
This makes the judge appear reliable on accuracy metrics while completely missing failure cases.
...
**Use LLM-as-Judge:**
- Factual question-answering with reference answers available
- Code correctness when execution environments are unavailable (use reasoning models as judge)
- Task completion assessment for multi-step agent workflows (binary or ternary scoring)
- Instruction adherence when rubrics can be precisely specified
- Pairwise A/B comparison between agent versions during deployment decisions
**Use deterministic checks instead:**
- Format validation (JSON schema, regex-matchable outputs)
- Calculation correctness (math, code that can be executed and tested)
- Retrieval precision (retrieved chunks contain the answer span)
**Use human evaluation (supplemented by LLM-as-Judge):**
- Creative quality, style, and tone
- Nuanced helpfulness judgments
...
**Do not use LLM-as-Judge alone:**
...
Grade inflation is not inevitable — it is a calibration failure.
Here is the protocol that production teams use to prevent it:
**Step 1: Build a calibration set.** Collect 200-500 human-labeled examples spanning the full quality distribution: clearly excellent, clearly poor, and ambiguous cases in the middle.
Balance the distribution deliberately — most production datasets skew heavily toward acceptable outputs, which trains your judge to over-accept.
**Step 2: Score against your calibration set.** Run your judge prompt against the calibration set and measure Cohen's Kappa (for ordinal scales) or Krippendorff's Alpha against human labels.
Target: Kappa > 0.60 (substantial agreement).
Anything below 0.40 indicates the judge prompt needs revision before production use.
**Step 3: Use few-shot anchoring.** Include 3-5 examples in your judge prompt with the correct verdict and rationale — one clearly good, one clearly bad, one borderline.
Few-shot calibration reduces verbosity bias and agreeableness bias more reliably than prompt instructions alone.
**Step 4: Apply narrow scales with behavioral anchors.** Replace 1-10 scales with 1-4 scales where each level has a concrete behavioral definition:
- **1**: Factually wrong, harmful, or completely off-task
- **2**: Partially correct but missing key information or requiring significant correction
- **3**: Correct and complete; minor style or efficiency improvements possible
- **4**: Correct, complete, and exemplary — would publish without changes
**Step 5: Run ensemble scoring for critical decisions.** For evaluations that gate production deployment or drive model procurement decisions, use 3-5 diverse judge models (GPT-5.4, Claude Opus 4.6, Gemini 3.1 Pro) with majority vote.
Research shows this reduces bias artifacts 30-40% versus single-judge evaluation.
Cost increase: 3-5x — worth it for high-stakes decisions, not warranted for routine monitoring.
**Step 6: Monitor judge drift.** Re-run your calibration set monthly.
Judge model behavior changes with model updates, and what calibrated well in Q1 2026 may exhibit new biases after a model update.
Automate calibration checks as part of your evaluation infrastructure, not as a one-time setup step.
...
1. **Start with factual and task-completion metrics** — highest reliability, lowest calibration burden.
Add subjective dimensions only after your objective metrics are calibrated.
2. **Never skip the calibration set.** A 200-example calibration corpus takes 4-8 hours to build and will surface bias issues before they contaminate thousands of evaluations.
3. **Use cross-model judging for procurement decisions.** If you are evaluating Claude vs. GPT-5.4 for your agent stack, use a third model (Gemini 3.1 Pro) as the judge.
...
4. **Treat judge prompts as production code.** Version them, test them against your calibration set on every change, and monitor them for drift after model updates.
...
5. **Automate calibration checks in CI.** Run your calibration set as part of every evaluation pipeline deployment.

results.title: The Rise of Agent-as-a-Judge Evaluation for LLMs - arXiv
results.snippet:
By bringing together these strands, our review demonstrates how agent-based judging can complement (but not replace) human oversight, marking a step toward trustworthy, scalable evaluation for next-generation LLMs.
...
Pointwise evaluation: The LLM judge scores one output at a time on given criteria (e.g. giving a coherence score) Kim et al.
...
Pairwise evaluation: The LLM judge compares two outputs and decides which is better Saad-Falcon et al.
(2024); Cao et al.
(2024).
This is used in win-rate metrics, where the judge picks a winner between model A vs B for the same query.
...
Early works like G-Eval Liu et al.
(2023) demonstrated that an LLM can perform surprisingly well in evaluating open-ended responses, achieving strong correlation with human raters on tasks like dialogue response quality.
In fact, automatic LLM-based benchmarks such as MT-Bench Zheng et al.
(2023) and AlpacaEval Li et al.
(2023) have reported high Spearman correlations (often 0.8–0.9) between GPT-4’s judgments and aggregate human preferences Zheng et al.
(2025).
...
Despite these efforts, single LLM judges have known biases and vulnerabilities Zheng et al.
(2023).
They can be gamed by adversarial outputs: recent research showed that even a nonsense “null” response can trick a GPT-4 evaluator into giving high rankings if crafted in a persuasive style Zheng et al.
...
This highlights that synergy among agents can correct individual model biases and yield more reliable scores.
...
Empirically, approaches like ChatEval, DEBATE, CourtEval, and MAJ-EVAL have all reported stronger agreement with human judgments and more robust evaluations than single-model methods.
...
In fact, the study reported that Agent-as-a-Judge dramatically outperformed a standard LLM-as-a-judge that only saw final outputs, and achieved parity with human evaluators on the code tasks.
For example, in one comparison, the agent judge’s decisions differed from the human-majority vote only  0.3% of the time, whereas a single LLM judge disagreed  31% of the time.
...
|LLM-as-a-Judge|Pointwise, pairwise, or checklist|Fast, scalable, transparent, good for general tasks|Susceptible to bias, style/preference lock-in, limited domain expertise|
...
|Agent-as-a-Judge|Step-by-step process and action evaluation|Granular feedback, tracks process not just outcome, close to human-level for agent tasks|Computational overhead, reproducibility, stability|
...
A well-calibrated LLM judge (especially GPT-4 class) shows impressively high agreement with aggregate human preferences (often in the 0.7–0.9 Spearman range) Liu et al.
...
Multi-Agent (Debate/Committee) Evaluators: By incorporating multiple agents and possibly adversarial roles, these frameworks generally achieve higher reliability and closer alignment to human consensus than a lone model.
For instance, both CourtEval Kumar et al.
(2025) and DEBATE Kim et al.
(2024a) demonstrated new state-of-the-art correlations with human judgments, beating prior single-model methods by a significant margin.
...
The Agent-as-a-Judge framework was shown to match human evaluators in reliability on code tasks, and even outperform individual human judges in some cases by being more consistent.
...
The downsides include computational cost (these judges often require running a full agent which might do web browsing, code execution, etc., incurring significant time per task).
...
Another consideration is stability: because agentic evaluations involve many moving parts (external tools, multi-step interactions), the results might have higher variance if any nondeterministic tool or API calls are involved.

First,
thresholding confidence scores offers no finite-sample sta-
tistical guarantee that a target accepted-set error will be re-
spected at deployment; thresholds tuned to match valida-
tion behavior can exceed the desired risk on new samples.
...
As a result, naive con-
fidence thresholding can fail to abstain precisely when it
should, violating a user’s reliability constraint even when
